channel.title: QE ArXiv Watch Weekly
channel.description: Weekly AI-synthesized digest of quality engineering research from arXiv. Covering Control Charts, Experimental Design, and Reliability Engineering.

item.title: QE ArXiv Watch: Week of April 13 - April 20, 2026
item.description:
The theme I couldn’t shake this week: we’re finally designing and monitoring systems the way they actually fail—sensors drop out, proxies lie in subgroups, and early-run control limits aren’t “asymptotic enough” to pretend otherwise.

Design that assumes the accelerometer will betray you

White et al. go after a problem anyone who’s instrumented a big structure recognizes: you plan a beautiful vibration test… and then a sensor clips, drops, or quietly dies.

What’s nice here is the practical robustness framing. Instead of designing for a single “ideal” Fisher information matrix, they design to do well on average across failure scenarios (including clipping treated as dropout), or via explicit per-sensor failure probabilities. And they make it computationally workable for a 267-candidate sensor set by relaxing binary “pick/not pick” decisions into continuous weights—then pushing those weights back toward 0/1 during optimization with a double-well penalty (so you don’t have to do crude rounding at the end).

Two things to take to practice:

  • If you can estimate dropout probabilities by location/environment, the probability-weighted robust objective changes the chosen sensors and improves average parameter/prediction MSE under many simulated failures.
  • If you can’t estimate probabilities, scenario-averaging is a clean alternative—and it naturally accommodates clipping scenarios.

Also worth a skeptical nod: in one “one-sensor-failure” setting, the robust and classical binary D-optimal designs coincided. Translation: sometimes D-optimality is already pretty robust. But the fractional solutions showed meaningful gains in worst/average loss when a “critical” sensor fails—exactly the kind of brittleness we see in the field.

Proxy metrics: stop asking “is it correlated?” and start asking “will it make the same decision?”

Amudala et al. (PROXIMA) is aimed at online experiments, but the idea generalizes: when the real outcome is slow, we ship based on a proxy. The proposal is a composite reliability score built from:
  • correlation of treatment effects (experiment-level),
  • directional accuracy (did the proxy get the sign right?),
  • and a fragility rate that flags segment-level sign reversals (Simpson’s paradox vibes).

The punchline is the fragility term. Two proxies can look “fine” in aggregate, yet one is a landmine in segments. In their sims, Criteo proxies look solid (low fragility), while KuaiRec shows huge fragility (segment sign flips are common) even when overall decision agreement is high. If you’ve ever had a metric that’s “good on average” but hurts a key cohort, you know why this belongs in a pre-ship checklist.

Reliability inference: dependent stress–strength and censored Weibull, with less hand-waving

Two more classical reliability contributions landed:

Kızılaslan et al. tackle dependent stress–strength R=P(X>Y) using modified Weibulls tied together with a Clayton copula (lower-tail dependence). It’s parameter-heavy, and R needs numerical integration, but the comparison of estimators is useful: ML (and often MPS) tends to win on MSE, while bootstrap intervals are safer than asymptotic ones when the delta-method math gets gnarly.

Liu et al. focus on Type-I censored Weibull data and build exact generalized inference by transforming to a Gumbel location–scale model and constructing generalized pivotal quantities. Their intervals land in the “Goldilocks zone”: WLMA is too conservative (comically high coverage, wide intervals), bootstrap under-covers, and this GPQ approach is closer to nominal with shorter intervals. If you live in censoring-heavy life tests, that’s immediately relevant.

SPC: adaptive limits that behave on day 1

Muritala et al. offer a nonparametric, multi-stream EWMA for binary monitoring by thresholding against the in-control median. The key engineering detail: they derive the exact time-varying variance of the EWMA statistic, so the control limits are valid from the first sample, not “eventually correct.” Detection looks strong for moderate shifts, and robustness across distributions is a real selling point when you’re tired of arguing about Normality.

One question to take into next week

Where else are we still designing “as if nothing breaks”? Sensors, proxies, censoring, early-run limits—these papers all treat failure modes as first-class citizens. If we did that consistently in our quality systems, which of our current “optimal” solutions would suddenly look fragile?

Featured Papers This Week

  • White et al.: Robust Optimal Experimental Design Accounting for Sensor Failure (Experimental Design)
  • Amudala et al.: PROXIMA: A Reliability Scoring Framework for Proxy Metrics in Online Controlled Experiments (Reliability)
  • Muritala et al.: A Nonparametric Adaptive EWMA Control Chart for Binary Monitoring of Multiple Stream Processes (Control Charts)
  • Kızılaslan et al.: Reliability estimation in dependent stress-strength model with Clayton copula and modified Weibull margins (Reliability)
  • Liu et al.: Inference on Survival Reliability with Type-I Censored Weibull data (Reliability)

Explore More: Visit the QE ArXiv Watch Dashboard to browse all papers with AI summaries, interactive filtering, and paper chat.

This digest is automatically generated every Monday. Questions or feedback? Open an issue on our GitHub repository.
